title: Analysis of spin avalanches due to interplay of disorder and temperature
abstract: The nonequilibrium zero-temperature Random Field Ising Model (RFIM) has been extensively studied to understand critical response and avalanches in disordered driven systems. The emergence of power-law behaviour is observed over a wide region around the critical point. These studies however, are confined to zero-temperature dynamics. We study the role of temperature, which is inevitable in real experiments, in the context of RFIM on triangular lattices. We explore the interplay of different parameters: temperature, random field strength, and relaxation time which affect the prevalence of power-law behaviour on the lattice. The results indicate that power-law survives only in the regime of low temperature or small and intermediate disorder. Variations in temperature and disorder have similar affects on the avalanche-size distribution, indicating their strong correspondence. We also discuss the process of blurring out of the power law on increasing temperature or disorder.
summary: This study examines how thermal fluctuations and disorder interact to influence the scaling behavior of spin avalanches in the Random Field Ising Model. It demonstrates that the characteristic power-law distributions found in zero-temperature models are suppressed by high temperature or high disorder. The findings provide insight into the stability of critical behavior in real-world, finite-temperature magnetic systems.
main_problem: Investigating how the interplay between temperature and disorder affects the prevalence of power-law behavior in spin avalanches, bridging the gap between zero-temperature models and finite-temperature physical systems.
main_result: Power-law behavior survives only in regimes of low temperature or small to intermediate disorder; increasing temperature or disorder leads to the blurring of the power law and the development of a peaked distribution.
method: Monte Carlo simulations using Glauber dynamics on a large 2D triangular lattice, with results averaged over multiple disorder configurations.
model_or_system: The 2D Random Field Ising Model (RFIM) on a 999x999 triangular lattice with ferromagnetic nearest-neighbor interactions and a Gaussian random field.
key_observables: Magnetization, hysteresis loop width (coercivity), remanent magnetization, avalanche size distribution, and the power-law decay exponent (alpha).
important_parameters: Temperature (T), random field strength (sigma), relaxation time (t), and external magnetic field (Hext).
main_assumptions: The system follows Glauber dynamics in contact with a heat bath; the random field is sampled from a Gaussian distribution.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows hysteresis loop narrowing with increasing relaxation time; Fig 2 and 3 show the evolution of remanent magnetization; Fig 4 shows hysteresis loops at different temperatures; Fig 5 shows avalanche size distributions across different disorder and time scales.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the RFIM problem, describes the Monte Carlo methodology and model parameters, presents results regarding the effects of temperature, disorder, and relaxation time on magnetization and avalanches, and concludes with a comparison to experimental Barkhausen noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Hysteresis loops on 999×999 triangular lattice show- ing the effects of Monte-Carlo cycle, t= 100, 500, 1000, 10000 for different disorder σ and temperature T(= 1/K) regimes: a)Small disorder (σ = 1); Low T (K = 2) b)Large disorder (σ = 4); Low T (K = 2) c)Small disorder (σ = 1); High T (K = 0.5) d)Large disorder (σ = 4); High T (K = 0.5).

FIG. 2. Disorder-dependent evolution of remanent magneti- zation versus Monte-Carlo cycles, t for a 999 × 999 triangular lattice at: a)K = 2 and b)K = 0.5 for σ = 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8 aver- aged over 20 disorder realizations.
FIG. 3. Remanence magnetization vs t for different tempera- tures K = 0.005, 0.05, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 2, 5 on the lower hysteresis loop at: a)σ = 1 and b)σ = 4.
FIG. 4. Hysteresis on a 999 × 999 triangular lattice for dif- ferent disorders(σ = 0.5, 1, 4, 6, 8) at t = 50 for fixed temper- atures: a)K = 2 and b)K = 0.5.

FIG. 5. Probability distribution of avalanche sizes for a 999×999 triangular lattice at K = 2 of varying disorders(σ = 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8), obtained by averaging 1000 localized random field configurations for: a)t = 50 and b)t = 500.
